title: Wet-felted tea cosy
subtitle: A flat-resist merino panel shaped around a template and cut open to fit a 4-cup teapot
excerpt: Wet-felt two merino panels over a flat paper resist cut to the size of a 4-cup teapot. The pre-shrink panel measures roughly 28 x 22 cm; post-shrink it settles to 20 x 16 cm. The flat-resist method follows the standard tea-cosy construction taught across European felting guilds and produces a neat, seamless cosy with a snug fit.
sourceNotes: Observation-led from traditional wet felting; the flat-resist method follows the standard tea-cosy construction taught across European felting guilds.

glossaryTerms.definition: A template placed between two layers of wet wool roving during felting. The resist prevents the two layers from bonding together at the contact face, leaving an opening once it is removed. For a tea cosy, a flat card or plastic resist cut to the teapot's profile creates the interior cavity.

The final stage of wet felting in which the partly-bonded fibre is worked harder, typically by rolling or throwing, to compact the fibres into a dense, stable fabric. Fulling follows the initial felting stage and is what gives wet-felted items their firm hand-feel.

content.text: This tea cosy is built over a flat
resist

: a card template cut to the profile of a 4-cup teapot. Merino roving is layered around the resist in cross-hatched passes, wet down, and felted on both faces. Once the fabric is firm, the resist is pulled free and the bottom edge is cut open, giving a snug cosy with no seams at the crown. The pre-shrink panel is approximately 28 x 22 cm; wet felting shrinks merino by 25 to 35 percent, so the finished cosy settles to around 20 x 16 cm, which fits a standard 4-cup teapot with a small amount of ease. Sage green, dusty mauve, or cream merino all felt cleanly and hold their colour without fading.

attrs.title: Slip hazard
attrs.body: Work on a non-slip surface. Hot soapy water on a smooth floor is a slip hazard; mop spills as they happen.

content.text: What you need

items.name: Merino roving in sage green, dusty mauve, or cream
Olive-oil soap (grated or liquid), for the soapy water
Hot water (as hot as your hands can bear)
Cold water (for shocking and rinsing)
items.qty: For a second bowl or tap

items.name: Card or thick plastic sheet, cut to your teapot's profile (trace around the teapot from the side, adding 3 cm all around for shrinkage buffer)
Felting mat or bubble-wrap mat (bubbles facing up)
items.qty: 1, large enough to lay the cosy flat

items.name: Felting net or old net curtain, to hold fibres in place during wetting
Bamboo rolling mat, for the fulling stage
Cotton tea-towel or old towel, for pressing out excess water
Soft tape measure, for checking shrinkage
Sharp scissors, for trimming the bottom edge

content.text: Method
Step 1: Make the resist template

Stand your teapot on a sheet of card. Draw around the side profile: from the base, up over the lid, and back down. The line should follow the widest point of the spout side and the handle side. Remove the teapot and add 3 cm beyond your drawn line all the way around. This buffer compensates for shrinkage. Cut the resist out cleanly. A resist made from a plastic folder or a sheet of thin foam board holds its shape better than plain card when wet.
Step 2: Lay out the first face of roving

Lay the felting mat on a table. Place the resist on the mat. Pull thin wisps from the roving and lay them in horizontal passes across the resist face, extending each wisp roughly 4 cm beyond the resist edge. Work in four layers: two horizontal, two vertical, each at 90 degrees to the last. Aim for even coverage without gaps or thick ridges. This is the cross-hatch principle: fibres running in two perpendicular directions interlock far more strongly during felting than a single-direction layer. Once four layers are down, gently fold the overhanging fibres back over the resist edge onto the opposite face.
Step 3: Lay out the second face and cover

Flip the resist over carefully, keeping the first-face fibres in place. Lay a further four cross-hatched layers on this second face in the same way: two horizontal, two vertical. Fold the overhanging fibres back over the edge as before. Both faces now have four layers each, plus the folded edges securing the perimeter. Lay the felting net over the top face to hold the fibres in place before wetting.
Step 4: Wet out and begin rubbing

Mix hot water and olive-oil soap in the large bowl. The water should be as hot as your hands can comfortably bear, around 50 to 60 degrees C. Carefully drizzle the soapy water over the net, a little at a time, pressing down gently with your flat palm between additions to saturate the roving without displacing the fibres. Once the whole face is saturated and the fibres have started to mat through the net, begin rubbing in firm, straight passes: across the width for 30 passes, then along the length for 30 passes. After 5 minutes of rubbing, lift the net, check the surface is holding together, re-cover, and rub for a further 5 minutes. The fibres should feel slightly stiffer and more cohesive than loose roving.
Step 5: Flip and repeat on the second face

Remove the net, slide your hand under the mat, and flip the entire assembly so the second face is now on top. Re-lay the net and repeat the wetting and rubbing process on this face: 10 minutes of firm rubbing in two passes, checking cohesion halfway through. By the end of this stage both faces should feel lightly bonded but still slightly spongy. Add more hot soapy water if the surface dries or cools during rubbing.
Step 6: Full to size
Once both faces are holding together, the piece is ready for
fulling

. Lay the bamboo rolling mat flat. Place the cosy on top of the mat (still with the resist inside), roll the mat loosely around the piece, and roll back and forth with firm palm pressure for 3 to 4 minutes. Unroll, rotate the piece 90 degrees, and roll again. Check the dimensions against your resist template: the cosy should be shrinking toward the post-shrink size of 20 x 16 cm. Shock the piece with cold water by dunking it briefly in the second bowl or running it under a cold tap, then return to the hot soapy water and roll again. Alternate hot and cold treatments two or three more times; each shock encourages the fibres to tighten further.
Step 7: Remove the resist and cut open

When the cosy has reached the finished dimensions and the fabric feels firm rather than spongy when squeezed, remove the resist. The bottom edge of the cosy will be closed by the folded fibres. Use sharp scissors to cut a straight line across the bottom edge, roughly 1.5 cm in from the fold. This opening allows the cosy to slide over the teapot. Check the fit while the cosy is still damp: it should slip over the teapot with a little resistance. If the opening is too tight, trim a further 1 cm. Rinse thoroughly in lukewarm water, gently press out excess water in a clean tea-towel, and reshape by hand over the teapot to dry.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The two faces have not bonded and the resist pulls the layers apart when removed
items.cause: Insufficient rubbing time on one or both faces, or the soapy water cooled too much before the fibre bonded
items.fix: If the faces feel loose at the end of Step 5, do not pull out the resist. Continue rubbing both faces for a further 5 minutes each with freshly heated soapy water. The fibre must feel cohesive and slightly stiff before fulling begins.

items.problem: The cosy has shrunk unevenly and is wider at one end than the other
items.cause: Uneven layer thickness, or the rolling mat was not rotated 90 degrees between fulling passes
items.fix: Rotate the piece 90 degrees between every rolling pass. If one area is noticeably thicker, concentrate extra rubbing there during the next hot-water stage. Mild unevenness in a finished cosy is normal; severe distortion usually means one section had twice the roving of another.

items.problem: The opening left after cutting is ragged or the edge begins to fray
items.cause: The cut was made before the fabric was fully felted, or the scissors compressed the still-wet edge
items.fix: Ensure the cosy has fully felted and reached its finished dimensions before cutting. Use sharp scissors and cut in one clean motion. If the cut edge feels loose, wet it with a little soapy water and rub it between your palms for 30 seconds to consolidate the raw edge before rinsing.

content.text: Finishing and care

Once the cosy is rinsed and pressed free of excess water in a tea-towel, reshape it over the teapot and leave it to dry at room temperature away from direct heat. The felt will harden slightly as it dries and hold the teapot's shape. To wash, hand-wash in cool water with a small amount of wool wash; do not wring or rub, and keep it out of the machine. Press out excess water gently and dry flat or over the teapot. Avoid prolonged exposure to direct sunlight, which fades natural wool dyes over time.
